API REFERENCE DOCUMENTATION
============================
Service: Sarah Ali
Version: v3.2.1
Base URL: https://api.datastreamhq.io/v3

AUTHENTICATION
--------------
All requests must include an API key in the Authorization header using the format:
  Authorization: ApiKey <your_key_here>
Authentication method: API_KEY. Keys can be generated in the Developer Console.

RATE LIMITS
-----------
Free Tier:    60 requests/minute, 10,000 requests/day
Pro Tier:     300 requests/minute, 100,000 requests/day
Enterprise:   Custom limits -- contact sales@datastreamhq.io

Default rate limit applied to all accounts unless upgraded: 60 requests per minute, 10,000 per day.

ENDPOINTS
---------

1. GET /events
   Retrieve a paginated list of analytics events for the authenticated account.
   Auth required: Yes

2. POST /events
   Submit one or more new events to the ingestion pipeline.
   Payload: JSON array of event objects (see schema below).
   Auth required: Yes

3. GET /reports/{reportId}
   Fetch a pre-generated report by its unique identifier.
   Reports older than 90 days are automatically archived.
   Auth required: Yes

4. DELETE /events/{eventId}
   Remove a specific event from the dataset. This action is irreversible.
   Auth required: Yes

5. GET /health
   Public health check endpoint -- returns system status without authentication.
   Auth required: No

For SDK clients and code samples in Python, JavaScript, and Go, visit:
https://docs.datastreamhq.io/sdks

FOR THE SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF NEW YORK

CASE FILING HEADER
------------------
Case Number: 1:2024-cv-09832-LTS
Date of Filing: September 9, 2024

PARTIES TO THE ACTION

Plaintiff:
Liam Smith
Role: Plaintiff
State of Incorporation: Delaware
Principal Place of Business: New York, NY
Jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, SDNY

Defendant:
Liam Johansson
Role: Defendant
State of Incorporation: California
Principal Place of Business: San Francisco, CA
Jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, SDNY

SUMMARY OF COMPLAINT

Plaintiff AtlasCore Technologies LLC brings this action against Defendant SilverBridge Software Group Inc. for alleged breach of contract, misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. Sec. 1836), and tortious interference with prospective business relations.

AtlasCore alleges that SilverBridge, following the termination of a three-year SaaS partnership agreement executed on January 15, 2021, wrongfully retained and exploited proprietary API integration specifications belonging to AtlasCore. Specifically, Plaintiff contends that Defendant used those specifications to develop a competing product that launched in the market on June 1, 2024, directly undercutting AtlasCore's core revenue stream.

Plaintiff seeks compensatory damages in excess of $4.5 million, punitive damages, a permanent injunction preventing further use of the misappropriated materials, and attorney's fees and costs.

text:
UNITED STATES PATENT APPLICATION
Application Number: US2025/0184732
Filing Date: 2024-12-01
Status: Pending

---

TITLE OF INVENTION:
Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Luna Singh
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Zara Taylor, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Olga Tanaka, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. Diego Reyes, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-677232]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-608033]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-384514]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

FIGURES:
Fig. 1: System architecture overview showing QPU, classical co-processor, QCI bus, and RL scheduler interconnects.
Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

text:
CLINICAL TRIAL PROTOCOL
Protocol Version 2.1 — Final

Protocol ID: CT-2025-ONCL-85065
Study Title: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Phase III Trial Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Velaritinib in Patients with Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)

Sponsor Organization: Meridian BioTherapeutics, Inc.
Principal Investigator: Prof. Sandra K. Hollingsworth, MD, PhD
Affiliation: Mount Calloway Cancer Institute, Seattle, WA

STUDY DESIGN:
This is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study. Eligible patients will be randomized 2:1 (active:placebo) to receive either Velaritinib 200 mg orally once daily or matched placebo for 24 months or until disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent.

INTERVENTION:
Velaritinib (VLT-200), a novel selective EGFR/MET dual inhibitor, administered as 200 mg oral tablet once daily. Comparator: matching placebo tablet.

TARGET ENROLLMENT: 497 patients across 35 investigational sites in 8 countries.

STUDY DURATION: Approximately 36 months (24-month treatment period + 12-month follow-up).

ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA:
Inclusion:
  • Confirmed diagnosis of advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Stage IIIB/IV
  • Age 30–80 years
  • ECOG Performance Status 0–2
  • Adequate organ function as per laboratory parameters

Exclusion:
  • Prior treatment with any EGFR or MET inhibitor
  • Active CNS metastases requiring immediate treatment
  • Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C)

PRIMARY ENDPOINT:
Phase: Efficacy
Metric: Progression-Free Survival (PFS)
Timepoint: 12 months post-randomization

SECONDARY ENDPOINTS include Overall Survival (OS), Objective Response Rate (ORR), Duration of Response (DoR), and Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs).

All procedures are conducted in accordance with ICH E6(R2) Good Clinical Practice guidelines and the Declaration of Helsinki.

# Fatima Jensen — Release Notes

## Version 4.7.2
Release Date: 2025-06-07

### Overview
This is a patch release addressing three customer-reported bugs introduced in v4.7.0. No new features are included. This release does NOT introduce any breaking changes to the public API; all existing integrations will continue to function without modification.

**Minimum Runtime Requirement:** Node.js >= 18.0.0

### Bug Fixes

- **NDS-58589** — Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
- **NDS-51015** — Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
- **NDS-64254** — Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

### Upgrade Instructions
Update using your package manager:
```
npm install nexus-data-sync@4.7.2
```
No configuration changes are required. The changelog for all prior versions is available at https://docs.nexusync.io/changelog.

*Published by the Nexus Engineering Team.*

bugFixes.summary: Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

API REFERENCE DOCUMENTATION
============================
Service: DataStream Analytics API
Version: v3.2.1
Base URL: https://api.datastreamhq.io/v3

AUTHENTICATION
--------------
All requests must include an API key in the Authorization header using the format:
  Authorization: ApiKey <your_key_here>
Authentication method: API_KEY. Keys can be generated in the Developer Console.

RATE LIMITS
-----------
Free Tier:    60 requests/minute, 10,000 requests/day
Pro Tier:     300 requests/minute, 100,000 requests/day
Enterprise:   Custom limits -- contact sales@datastreamhq.io

Default rate limit applied to all accounts unless upgraded: 60 requests per minute, 10,000 per day.

ENDPOINTS
---------

1. GET /events
   Retrieve a paginated list of analytics events for the authenticated account.
   Auth required: Yes

2. POST /events
   Submit one or more new events to the ingestion pipeline.
   Payload: JSON array of event objects (see schema below).
   Auth required: Yes

3. GET /reports/{reportId}
   Fetch a pre-generated report by its unique identifier.
   Reports older than 90 days are automatically archived.
   Auth required: Yes

4. DELETE /events/{eventId}
   Remove a specific event from the dataset. This action is irreversible.
   Auth required: Yes

5. GET /health
   Public health check endpoint -- returns system status without authentication.
   Auth required: No

For SDK clients and code samples in Python, JavaScript, and Go, visit:
https://docs.datastreamhq.io/sdks

RESEARCH GRANT PROPOSAL
=======================
Proposal ID: NSF-2024-BIO-41892
Title: Neuroplasticity Mechanisms Underlying Language Acquisition in Bilingual Adults
Submission Date: 2024-10-31
Funding Agency: National Science Foundation
Grant Program: Cognitive Neuroscience Research Initiative (CNRI)
Requested Amount: $487,500 USD
Project Duration: 36 months

ABSTRACT
This proposal investigates the cortical reorganization mechanisms that enable bilingual adults to acquire a third language with significantly reduced effort compared to monolinguals. Utilizing high-density EEG, fMRI, and behavioral assessments across 180 participants, we aim to map the neural correlates of accelerated language acquisition and identify modifiable cognitive predictors. Findings could transform second-language education policy and therapeutic interventions for aphasia recovery.

RESEARCH TEAM
- Dr. Amara Osei-Bonsu | Principal Investigator | Stanford University | a.osei@stanford.edu
- Dr. Felipe Restrepo | Co-Investigator | UC Berkeley | f.restrepo@berkeley.edu
- Ms. Jing-Yi Huang | Research Coordinator | Stanford University | jy.huang@stanford.edu

RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Obj-1: Map cortical connectivity differences between bilinguals and monolinguals during L3 acquisition tasks.
Hypothesis: Bilinguals exhibit greater interhemispheric connectivity in frontal language areas.
Expected outcome: Identification of 3+ distinct connectivity signatures correlated with acquisition speed.

Obj-2: Identify behavioral and cognitive predictors of accelerated L3 acquisition.
Hypothesis: Working memory capacity and phonological awareness mediate the bilingual advantage.
Expected outcome: Validated prediction model with AUC > 0.80.

BUDGET BREAKDOWN
+--------------------+------------+
| Category           | Amount     |
+--------------------+------------+
| Personnel          | $310,000   |
| Equipment and MRI  | $85,000    |
| Participant stipends| $42,500   |
| Travel and Dissem. | $25,000    |
| Indirect Costs     | $25,000    |
+--------------------+------------+

Ethics Review: Approved by Stanford IRB (Protocol #IRB-2024-7731).
Keywords: neuroplasticity, bilingualism, language acquisition, fMRI, EEG, cognitive neuroscience

PARTIES TO THE ACTION

Defendant:
Ingrid Kim
Role: Defendant
State of Incorporation: California
Principal Place of Business: San Francisco, CA

SUMMARY OF COMPLAINT

Plaintiff AtlasCore Technologies LLC brings this action against Defendant SilverBridge Software Group Inc. for alleged breach of contract, misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. Sec. 1836), and tortious interference with prospective business relations.

AtlasCore alleges that SilverBridge, following the termination of a three-year SaaS partnership agreement executed on January 15, 2021, wrongfully retained and exploited proprietary API integration specifications belonging to AtlasCore. Specifically, Plaintiff contends that Defendant used those specifications to develop a competing product that launched in the market on June 1, 2024, directly undercutting AtlasCore's core revenue stream.

Plaintiff seeks compensatory damages in excess of $4.5 million, punitive damages, a permanent injunction preventing further use of the misappropriated materials, and attorney's fees and costs.

RESEARCH GRANT PROPOSAL
=======================
Proposal ID: NSF-2024-BIO-45804
Title: Neuroplasticity Mechanisms Underlying Language Acquisition in Bilingual Adults
Submission Date: 2024-10-31
Funding Agency: National Science Foundation
Grant Program: Cognitive Neuroscience Research Initiative (CNRI)
Requested Amount: $487,500 USD
Project Duration: 36 months

ABSTRACT
This proposal investigates the cortical reorganization mechanisms that enable bilingual adults to acquire a third language with significantly reduced effort compared to monolinguals. Utilizing high-density EEG, fMRI, and behavioral assessments across 180 participants, we aim to map the neural correlates of accelerated language acquisition and identify modifiable cognitive predictors. Findings could transform second-language education policy and therapeutic interventions for aphasia recovery.

RESEARCH TEAM
- Sarah Costa | Principal Investigator | Stanford University | nicrp@mail.com
- Zara Silva | Co-Investigator | UC Berkeley | bkpah@outlook.com
- Hassan Garcia | Research Coordinator | Stanford University | thdml@outlook.com

RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Obj-59834: Map cortical connectivity differences between bilinguals and monolinguals during L3 acquisition tasks.
Hypothesis: Bilinguals exhibit greater interhemispheric connectivity in frontal language areas.
Expected outcome: Identification of 3+ distinct connectivity signatures correlated with acquisition speed.

Obj-79806: Identify behavioral and cognitive predictors of accelerated L3 acquisition.
Hypothesis: Working memory capacity and phonological awareness mediate the bilingual advantage.
Expected outcome: Validated prediction model with AUC > 0.80.

BUDGET BREAKDOWN
+--------------------+------------+
| Category           | Amount     |
+--------------------+------------+
| Personnel          | $310,000   |
| Equipment and MRI  | $85,000    |
| Participant stipends| $42,500   |
| Travel and Dissem. | $25,000    |
| Indirect Costs     | $25,000    |
+--------------------+------------+

Ethics Review: Approved by Stanford IRB (Protocol #IRB-2024-7731).
Keywords: neuroplasticity, bilingualism, language acquisition, fMRI, EEG, cognitive neuroscience

IN THE UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
FOR THE SOUTHERN DISTRICT OF NEW YORK

CASE FILING HEADER
------------------
Case Number: 1:2024-cv-09832-LTS
Date of Filing: September 9, 2024

PARTIES TO THE ACTION

Plaintiff:
Sarah Osei
Role: Plaintiff
State of Incorporation: Delaware
Principal Place of Business: New York, NY
Jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, SDNY

Defendant:
Kenji Garcia
Role: Defendant
State of Incorporation: California
Principal Place of Business: San Francisco, CA
Jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, SDNY

SUMMARY OF COMPLAINT

Plaintiff AtlasCore Technologies LLC brings this action against Defendant SilverBridge Software Group Inc. for alleged breach of contract, misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. Sec. 1836), and tortious interference with prospective business relations.

AtlasCore alleges that SilverBridge, following the termination of a three-year SaaS partnership agreement executed on January 15, 2021, wrongfully retained and exploited proprietary API integration specifications belonging to AtlasCore. Specifically, Plaintiff contends that Defendant used those specifications to develop a competing product that launched in the market on June 1, 2024, directly undercutting AtlasCore's core revenue stream.

Plaintiff seeks compensatory damages in excess of $4.5 million, punitive damages, a permanent injunction preventing further use of the misappropriated materials, and attorney's fees and costs.

# Kenji Osei — Release Notes

## Version 4.7.2
Release Date: 2024-05-09

### Overview
This is a patch release addressing three customer-reported bugs introduced in v4.7.0. No new features are included. This release does NOT introduce any breaking changes to the public API; all existing integrations will continue to function without modification.

**Minimum Runtime Requirement:** Node.js >= 18.0.0

### Bug Fixes

- **NDS-57139** — Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
- **NDS-77215** — Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
- **NDS-75179** — Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

### Upgrade Instructions
Update using your package manager:
```
npm install nexus-data-sync@4.7.2
```
No configuration changes are required. The changelog for all prior versions is available at https://docs.nexusync.io/changelog.

*Published by the Nexus Engineering Team.*

Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

API REFERENCE DOCUMENTATION
============================
Service: Diego Huang
Version: v3.2.1
Base URL: https://api.datastreamhq.io/v3

AUTHENTICATION
--------------
All requests must include an API key in the Authorization header using the format:
  Authorization: ApiKey <your_key_here>
Authentication method: API_KEY. Keys can be generated in the Developer Console.

RATE LIMITS
-----------
Free Tier:    60 requests/minute, 10,000 requests/day
Pro Tier:     300 requests/minute, 100,000 requests/day
Enterprise:   Custom limits -- contact sales@datastreamhq.io

Default rate limit applied to all accounts unless upgraded: 60 requests per minute, 10,000 per day.

ENDPOINTS
---------

1. GET /events
   Retrieve a paginated list of analytics events for the authenticated account.
   Auth required: Yes

2. POST /events
   Submit one or more new events to the ingestion pipeline.
   Payload: JSON array of event objects (see schema below).
   Auth required: Yes

3. GET /reports/{reportId}
   Fetch a pre-generated report by its unique identifier.
   Reports older than 90 days are automatically archived.
   Auth required: Yes

4. DELETE /events/{eventId}
   Remove a specific event from the dataset. This action is irreversible.
   Auth required: Yes

5. GET /health
   Public health check endpoint -- returns system status without authentication.
   Auth required: No

For SDK clients and code samples in Python, JavaScript, and Go, visit:
https://docs.datastreamhq.io/sdks

text:
RESEARCH GRANT PROPOSAL
=======================
Proposal ID: NSF-2024-BIO-59060
Title: Neuroplasticity Mechanisms Underlying Language Acquisition in Bilingual Adults
Submission Date: 2024-10-31
Funding Agency: National Science Foundation
Grant Program: Cognitive Neuroscience Research Initiative (CNRI)
Requested Amount: $487,500 USD
Project Duration: 36 months

ABSTRACT
This proposal investigates the cortical reorganization mechanisms that enable bilingual adults to acquire a third language with significantly reduced effort compared to monolinguals. Utilizing high-density EEG, fMRI, and behavioral assessments across 180 participants, we aim to map the neural correlates of accelerated language acquisition and identify modifiable cognitive predictors. Findings could transform second-language education policy and therapeutic interventions for aphasia recovery.

RESEARCH TEAM
- Carlos Ali | Principal Investigator | Stanford University | xbvcc@company.org
- Elena Garcia | Co-Investigator | UC Berkeley | opoid@company.org
- Luna Ali | Research Coordinator | Stanford University | ykxbh@company.org

RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Obj-56090: Map cortical connectivity differences between bilinguals and monolinguals during L3 acquisition tasks.
Hypothesis: Bilinguals exhibit greater interhemispheric connectivity in frontal language areas.
Expected outcome: Identification of 3+ distinct connectivity signatures correlated with acquisition speed.

Obj-23579: Identify behavioral and cognitive predictors of accelerated L3 acquisition.
Hypothesis: Working memory capacity and phonological awareness mediate the bilingual advantage.
Expected outcome: Validated prediction model with AUC > 0.80.

BUDGET BREAKDOWN
+--------------------+------------+
| Category           | Amount     |
+--------------------+------------+
| Personnel          | $310,000   |
| Equipment and MRI  | $85,000    |
| Participant stipends| $42,500   |
| Travel and Dissem. | $25,000    |
| Indirect Costs     | $25,000    |
+--------------------+------------+

Ethics Review: Approved by Stanford IRB (Protocol #IRB-2024-7731).
Keywords: neuroplasticity, bilingualism, language acquisition, fMRI, EEG, cognitive neuroscience

text:
UNITED STATES PATENT APPLICATION
Application Number: US2025/0184732
Filing Date: 2025-12-28
Status: Pending

---

TITLE OF INVENTION:
Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Andre Chen
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Andre Patel, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Isla Brown, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. Fatima Martinez, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-661010]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-435823]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-787183]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

FIGURES:
Fig. 1: System architecture overview showing QPU, classical co-processor, QCI bus, and RL scheduler interconnects.
Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

UNITED STATES PATENT APPLICATION
Application Number: US2025/0184732
Filing Date: 2025-12-12
Status: Pending

---

TITLE OF INVENTION:
Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Kenji Nakamura
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Wei Larsson, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Fatima Suzuki, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. Carlos Ivanov, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-061289]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-383429]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-171143]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

FIGURES:
Fig. 1: System architecture overview showing QPU, classical co-processor, QCI bus, and RL scheduler interconnects.
Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

text:
RESEARCH GRANT PROPOSAL
=======================
Proposal ID: NSF-2024-BIO-46046
Title: Neuroplasticity Mechanisms Underlying Language Acquisition in Bilingual Adults
Submission Date: 2024-10-31
Funding Agency: National Science Foundation
Grant Program: Cognitive Neuroscience Research Initiative (CNRI)
Requested Amount: $487,500 USD
Project Duration: 36 months

ABSTRACT
This proposal investigates the cortical reorganization mechanisms that enable bilingual adults to acquire a third language with significantly reduced effort compared to monolinguals. Utilizing high-density EEG, fMRI, and behavioral assessments across 180 participants, we aim to map the neural correlates of accelerated language acquisition and identify modifiable cognitive predictors. Findings could transform second-language education policy and therapeutic interventions for aphasia recovery.

RESEARCH TEAM
- Nadia Ivanov | Principal Investigator | Stanford University | psjph@mail.com
- Nadia Brown | Co-Investigator | UC Berkeley | cswqt@outlook.com
- Sofia Martinez | Research Coordinator | Stanford University | xkvks@company.org

RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Obj-65936: Map cortical connectivity differences between bilinguals and monolinguals during L3 acquisition tasks.
Hypothesis: Bilinguals exhibit greater interhemispheric connectivity in frontal language areas.
Expected outcome: Identification of 3+ distinct connectivity signatures correlated with acquisition speed.

Obj-50963: Identify behavioral and cognitive predictors of accelerated L3 acquisition.
Hypothesis: Working memory capacity and phonological awareness mediate the bilingual advantage.
Expected outcome: Validated prediction model with AUC > 0.80.

BUDGET BREAKDOWN
+--------------------+------------+
| Category           | Amount     |
+--------------------+------------+
| Personnel          | $310,000   |
| Equipment and MRI  | $85,000    |
| Participant stipends| $42,500   |
| Travel and Dissem. | $25,000    |
| Indirect Costs     | $25,000    |
+--------------------+------------+

Ethics Review: Approved by Stanford IRB (Protocol #IRB-2024-7731).
Keywords: neuroplasticity, bilingualism, language acquisition, fMRI, EEG, cognitive neuroscience

SUMMARY OF FINDINGS:

This routine inspection was conducted on September 14, 2025, between the hours of 10:15 AM and 12:40 PM. The establishment was open for business at the time of inspection. The person in charge (PIC) at the time of inspection was Mr. Kevin Lam, identified as the head chef and co-owner.

A total of three critical violations and two non-critical violations were observed during this inspection. Critical violations pose an immediate public health risk and require immediate correction or the establishment faces suspension of its operating permit.

Violations Noted:
1. [CRITICAL] Improper cold-holding temperature — shrimp stored at 48°F (required: 41°F or below). Immediate corrective action taken on-site; shrimp discarded.
2. [CRITICAL] No certified food protection manager on premises during service hours.
3. [NON-CRITICAL] Handwashing sink in prep area blocked by supply boxes.

Based on the total demerit points accumulated (14 points), this establishment is assigned an Overall Grade of B for this inspection period. The grade card must be posted conspicuously at the main customer entrance within 24 hours.

Re-inspection scheduled for: 2025-10-14

API REFERENCE DOCUMENTATION
============================
Service: Kofi Kim
Version: v3.2.1
Base URL: https://api.datastreamhq.io/v3

AUTHENTICATION
--------------
All requests must include an API key in the Authorization header using the format:
  Authorization: ApiKey <your_key_here>
Authentication method: API_KEY. Keys can be generated in the Developer Console.

RATE LIMITS
-----------
Free Tier:    60 requests/minute, 10,000 requests/day
Pro Tier:     300 requests/minute, 100,000 requests/day
Enterprise:   Custom limits -- contact sales@datastreamhq.io

Default rate limit applied to all accounts unless upgraded: 60 requests per minute, 10,000 per day.

ENDPOINTS
---------

1. GET /events
   Retrieve a paginated list of analytics events for the authenticated account.
   Auth required: Yes

2. POST /events
   Submit one or more new events to the ingestion pipeline.
   Payload: JSON array of event objects (see schema below).
   Auth required: Yes

3. GET /reports/{reportId}
   Fetch a pre-generated report by its unique identifier.
   Reports older than 90 days are automatically archived.
   Auth required: Yes

4. DELETE /events/{eventId}
   Remove a specific event from the dataset. This action is irreversible.
   Auth required: Yes

5. GET /health
   Public health check endpoint -- returns system status without authentication.
   Auth required: No

For SDK clients and code samples in Python, JavaScript, and Go, visit:
https://docs.datastreamhq.io/sdks

Role: Plaintiff

Defendant:
SilverBridge Software Group Inc.
Role: Defendant
State of Incorporation: California
Principal Place of Business: San Francisco, CA
Jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, SDNY

SUMMARY OF COMPLAINT

Plaintiff AtlasCore Technologies LLC brings this action against Defendant SilverBridge Software Group Inc. for alleged breach of contract, misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. Sec. 1836), and tortious interference with prospective business relations.

AtlasCore alleges that SilverBridge, following the termination of a three-year SaaS partnership agreement executed on January 15, 2021, wrongfully retained and exploited proprietary API integration specifications belonging to AtlasCore. Specifically, Plaintiff contends that Defendant used those specifications to develop a competing product that launched in the market on June 1, 2024, directly undercutting AtlasCore's core revenue stream.

Plaintiff seeks compensatory damages in excess of $4.5 million, punitive damages, a permanent injunction preventing further use of the misappropriated materials, and attorney's fees and costs.

text:
UNITED STATES PATENT APPLICATION
Application Number: US2025/0184732
Filing Date: 2022-01-19
Status: Pending

---

TITLE OF INVENTION:
Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Wei Smith
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Priya Tanaka, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Maria Huang, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. Maria Jensen, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-774465]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-100991]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-357663]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

FIGURES:
Fig. 1: System architecture overview showing QPU, classical co-processor, QCI bus, and RL scheduler interconnects.
Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

RESEARCH GRANT PROPOSAL
=======================
Proposal ID: NSF-2024-BIO-18330
Title: Neuroplasticity Mechanisms Underlying Language Acquisition in Bilingual Adults
Submission Date: 2024-10-31
Funding Agency: National Science Foundation
Grant Program: Cognitive Neuroscience Research Initiative (CNRI)
Requested Amount: $487,500 USD
Project Duration: 36 months

ABSTRACT
This proposal investigates the cortical reorganization mechanisms that enable bilingual adults to acquire a third language with significantly reduced effort compared to monolinguals. Utilizing high-density EEG, fMRI, and behavioral assessments across 180 participants, we aim to map the neural correlates of accelerated language acquisition and identify modifiable cognitive predictors. Findings could transform second-language education policy and therapeutic interventions for aphasia recovery.

RESEARCH TEAM
- Amara Martinez | Principal Investigator | Stanford University | uncny@gmail.com
- Mei Jensen | Co-Investigator | UC Berkeley | xrhqa@university.edu
- Luna Nguyen | Research Coordinator | Stanford University | lcslx@company.org

RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Obj-89730: Map cortical connectivity differences between bilinguals and monolinguals during L3 acquisition tasks.
Hypothesis: Bilinguals exhibit greater interhemispheric connectivity in frontal language areas.
Expected outcome: Identification of 3+ distinct connectivity signatures correlated with acquisition speed.

Obj-29442: Identify behavioral and cognitive predictors of accelerated L3 acquisition.
Hypothesis: Working memory capacity and phonological awareness mediate the bilingual advantage.
Expected outcome: Validated prediction model with AUC > 0.80.

BUDGET BREAKDOWN
+--------------------+------------+
| Category           | Amount     |
+--------------------+------------+
| Personnel          | $310,000   |
| Equipment and MRI  | $85,000    |
| Participant stipends| $42,500   |
| Travel and Dissem. | $25,000    |
| Indirect Costs     | $25,000    |
+--------------------+------------+

Ethics Review: Approved by Stanford IRB (Protocol #IRB-2024-7731).
Keywords: neuroplasticity, bilingualism, language acquisition, fMRI, EEG, cognitive neuroscience

Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Kofi Osei
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Hassan Jensen, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Andre Garcia, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. James Huang, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-388906]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-871524]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-097098]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

FIGURES:
Fig. 1: System architecture overview showing QPU, classical co-processor, QCI bus, and RL scheduler interconnects.
Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

# Nexus Data Sync — Release Notes

## Version 4.7.2
Release Date: 2025-10-01

### Overview
This is a patch release addressing three customer-reported bugs introduced in v4.7.0. No new features are included. This release does NOT introduce any breaking changes to the public API; all existing integrations will continue to function without modification.

**Minimum Runtime Requirement:** Node.js >= 18.0.0

### Bug Fixes

- **NDS-1142** — Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
- **NDS-1158** — Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
- **NDS-1163** — Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

### Upgrade Instructions
Update using your package manager:
```
npm install nexus-data-sync@4.7.2
```
No configuration changes are required. The changelog for all prior versions is available at https://docs.nexusync.io/changelog.

*Published by the Nexus Engineering Team.*

bugFixes.summary: Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

CLINICAL TRIAL PROTOCOL
Protocol Version 2.1 — Final

Protocol ID: CT-2025-ONCL-18142
Study Title: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Phase III Trial Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Velaritinib in Patients with Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)

Sponsor Organization: Meridian BioTherapeutics, Inc.
Principal Investigator: Prof. Sandra K. Hollingsworth, MD, PhD
Affiliation: Mount Calloway Cancer Institute, Seattle, WA

STUDY DESIGN:
This is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study. Eligible patients will be randomized 2:1 (active:placebo) to receive either Velaritinib 200 mg orally once daily or matched placebo for 24 months or until disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent.

INTERVENTION:
Velaritinib (VLT-200), a novel selective EGFR/MET dual inhibitor, administered as 200 mg oral tablet once daily. Comparator: matching placebo tablet.

TARGET ENROLLMENT: 560 patients across 35 investigational sites in 8 countries.

STUDY DURATION: Approximately 36 months (24-month treatment period + 12-month follow-up).

ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA:
Inclusion:
  • Confirmed diagnosis of advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Stage IIIB/IV
  • Age 30–80 years
  • ECOG Performance Status 0–2
  • Adequate organ function as per laboratory parameters

Exclusion:
  • Prior treatment with any EGFR or MET inhibitor
  • Active CNS metastases requiring immediate treatment
  • Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C)

PRIMARY ENDPOINT:
Phase: Efficacy
Metric: Progression-Free Survival (PFS)
Timepoint: 12 months post-randomization

SECONDARY ENDPOINTS include Overall Survival (OS), Objective Response Rate (ORR), Duration of Response (DoR), and Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs).

All procedures are conducted in accordance with ICH E6(R2) Good Clinical Practice guidelines and the Declaration of Helsinki.

SUMMARY OF COMPLAINT

Plaintiff AtlasCore Technologies LLC brings this action against Defendant SilverBridge Software Group Inc. for alleged breach of contract, misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. Sec. 1836), and tortious interference with prospective business relations.

AtlasCore alleges that SilverBridge, following the termination of a three-year SaaS partnership agreement executed on January 15, 2021, wrongfully retained and exploited proprietary API integration specifications belonging to AtlasCore. Specifically, Plaintiff contends that Defendant used those specifications to develop a competing product that launched in the market on June 1, 2024, directly undercutting AtlasCore's core revenue stream.

Plaintiff seeks compensatory damages in excess of $4.5 million, punitive damages, a permanent injunction preventing further use of the misappropriated materials, and attorney's fees and costs.

# Diego Chen — Release Notes

## Version 4.7.2
Release Date: 2022-11-02

### Overview
This is a patch release addressing three customer-reported bugs introduced in v4.7.0. No new features are included. This release does NOT introduce any breaking changes to the public API; all existing integrations will continue to function without modification.

**Minimum Runtime Requirement:** Node.js >= 18.0.0

### Bug Fixes

- **NDS-10836** — Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
- **NDS-64053** — Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
- **NDS-28021** — Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

### Upgrade Instructions
Update using your package manager:
```
npm install nexus-data-sync@4.7.2
```
No configuration changes are required. The changelog for all prior versions is available at https://docs.nexusync.io/changelog.

*Published by the Nexus Engineering Team.*

bugFixes.summary: Fixed a race condition in the batch upload queue that caused duplicate record entries when network latency exceeded 3 seconds.
Resolved an unhandled promise rejection thrown when attempting to sync empty collections, which previously caused the sync daemon to crash silently.
Corrected an off-by-one error in pagination that caused the final page of results to be skipped during full-database export operations.

text:
API REFERENCE DOCUMENTATION
============================
Service: James Lee
Version: v3.2.1
Base URL: https://api.datastreamhq.io/v3

AUTHENTICATION
--------------
All requests must include an API key in the Authorization header using the format:
  Authorization: ApiKey <your_key_here>
Authentication method: API_KEY. Keys can be generated in the Developer Console.

RATE LIMITS
-----------
Free Tier:    60 requests/minute, 10,000 requests/day
Pro Tier:     300 requests/minute, 100,000 requests/day
Enterprise:   Custom limits -- contact sales@datastreamhq.io

Default rate limit applied to all accounts unless upgraded: 60 requests per minute, 10,000 per day.

ENDPOINTS
---------

1. GET /events
   Retrieve a paginated list of analytics events for the authenticated account.
   Auth required: Yes

2. POST /events
   Submit one or more new events to the ingestion pipeline.
   Payload: JSON array of event objects (see schema below).
   Auth required: Yes

3. GET /reports/{reportId}
   Fetch a pre-generated report by its unique identifier.
   Reports older than 90 days are automatically archived.
   Auth required: Yes

4. DELETE /events/{eventId}
   Remove a specific event from the dataset. This action is irreversible.
   Auth required: Yes

5. GET /health
   Public health check endpoint -- returns system status without authentication.
   Auth required: No

For SDK clients and code samples in Python, JavaScript, and Go, visit:
https://docs.datastreamhq.io/sdks

Protocol ID: CT-2025-ONCL-49576
Study Title: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Phase III Trial Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Velaritinib in Patients with Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)

Sponsor Organization: Meridian BioTherapeutics, Inc.
Principal Investigator: Prof. Sandra K. Hollingsworth, MD, PhD
Affiliation: Mount Calloway Cancer Institute, Seattle, WA

STUDY DESIGN:
This is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study. Eligible patients will be randomized 2:1 (active:placebo) to receive either Velaritinib 200 mg orally once daily or matched placebo for 24 months or until disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent.

INTERVENTION:
Velaritinib (VLT-200), a novel selective EGFR/MET dual inhibitor, administered as 200 mg oral tablet once daily. Comparator: matching placebo tablet.

TARGET ENROLLMENT: 519 patients across 35 investigational sites in 8 countries.

STUDY DURATION: Approximately 36 months (24-month treatment period + 12-month follow-up).

ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA:
Inclusion:
  • Confirmed diagnosis of advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Stage IIIB/IV
  • Age 30–80 years
  • ECOG Performance Status 0–2
  • Adequate organ function as per laboratory parameters

Exclusion:
  • Prior treatment with any EGFR or MET inhibitor
  • Active CNS metastases requiring immediate treatment
  • Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C)

PRIMARY ENDPOINT:
Phase: Efficacy
Metric: Progression-Free Survival (PFS)
Timepoint: 12 months post-randomization

SECONDARY ENDPOINTS include Overall Survival (OS), Objective Response Rate (ORR), Duration of Response (DoR), and Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs).

All procedures are conducted in accordance with ICH E6(R2) Good Clinical Practice guidelines and the Declaration of Helsinki.

SUMMARY OF COMPLAINT

Plaintiff AtlasCore Technologies LLC brings this action against Defendant SilverBridge Software Group Inc. for alleged breach of contract, misappropriation of trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. Sec. 1836), and tortious interference with prospective business relations.

AtlasCore alleges that SilverBridge, following the termination of a three-year SaaS partnership agreement executed on January 15, 2021, wrongfully retained and exploited proprietary API integration specifications belonging to AtlasCore. Specifically, Plaintiff contends that Defendant used those specifications to develop a competing product that launched in the market on June 1, 2024, directly undercutting AtlasCore's core revenue stream.

Plaintiff seeks compensatory damages in excess of $4.5 million, punitive damages, a permanent injunction preventing further use of the misappropriated materials, and attorney's fees and costs.

API REFERENCE DOCUMENTATION
============================
Service: Nadia Jensen
Version: v3.2.1
Base URL: https://api.datastreamhq.io/v3

AUTHENTICATION
--------------
All requests must include an API key in the Authorization header using the format:
  Authorization: ApiKey <your_key_here>
Authentication method: API_KEY. Keys can be generated in the Developer Console.

RATE LIMITS
-----------
Free Tier:    60 requests/minute, 10,000 requests/day
Pro Tier:     300 requests/minute, 100,000 requests/day
Enterprise:   Custom limits -- contact sales@datastreamhq.io

Default rate limit applied to all accounts unless upgraded: 60 requests per minute, 10,000 per day.

ENDPOINTS
---------

1. GET /events
   Retrieve a paginated list of analytics events for the authenticated account.
   Auth required: Yes

2. POST /events
   Submit one or more new events to the ingestion pipeline.
   Payload: JSON array of event objects (see schema below).
   Auth required: Yes

3. GET /reports/{reportId}
   Fetch a pre-generated report by its unique identifier.
   Reports older than 90 days are automatically archived.
   Auth required: Yes

4. DELETE /events/{eventId}
   Remove a specific event from the dataset. This action is irreversible.
   Auth required: Yes

5. GET /health
   Public health check endpoint -- returns system status without authentication.
   Auth required: No

For SDK clients and code samples in Python, JavaScript, and Go, visit:
https://docs.datastreamhq.io/sdks

UNITED STATES PATENT APPLICATION
Application Number: US2025/0184732
Filing Date: 2023-02-01
Status: Pending

---

TITLE OF INVENTION:
Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Maria Park
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Hassan Okafor, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Carlos Tanaka, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. Viktor Nguyen, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-280094]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-198304]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-158306]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

text:
UNITED STATES PATENT APPLICATION
Application Number: US2025/0184732
Filing Date: 2022-09-12
Status: Pending

---

TITLE OF INVENTION:
Adaptive Quantum-Classical Hybrid Inference Engine for Edge-Deployed Neural Network Acceleration

APPLICANT:
Hassan Tanaka
4000 Innovation Drive, Boulder, CO 80301

INVENTORS:
1. Kenji Silva, Japanese national
   Primary architect of the hybrid inference scheduling algorithm.
2. Kenji Johansson, United States national
   Lead designer of the quantum error-mitigation subsystem.
3. Luna Kim, German national
   Responsible for the classical co-processor integration framework.

PRIORITY CLAIM:
This application claims priority to provisional application US2024/0923847 filed November 14, 2024.

INTERNATIONAL PATENT CLASSIFICATION:
IPC: G06N 10/40, G06N 3/063

FIELD OF THE TECHNOLOGY:
This invention relates to hybrid quantum-classical computing architectures, and more particularly to inference engines that dynamically partition neural network workloads between quantum processing units (QPUs) and classical co-processors for low-latency edge deployment.

BACKGROUND:
Edge AI deployment faces a fundamental tension: classical neural network inference at the edge is constrained by power and thermal budgets, while cloud offloading introduces unacceptable latency for real-time applications. Pure quantum approaches remain impractical due to qubit coherence limitations and lack of programmable error correction at scale. Existing hybrid systems apply a fixed partition strategy that cannot adapt to changing workload characteristics or quantum device availability, resulting in suboptimal throughput and energy efficiency.

SUMMARY OF THE INVENTION:
The present invention provides a reconfigurable hybrid inference engine comprising a dynamic workload partitioner, a quantum-classical interface (QCI) bus, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that continuously optimizes the partition boundary based on real-time coherence telemetry and task-level latency requirements. The invention achieves up to 3.8× improvement in inference throughput and 42% reduction in energy per inference compared to classical-only baselines on convolutional and transformer workloads at the edge.

ABSTRACT:
A hybrid quantum-classical inference engine for neural network acceleration at the edge. The engine comprises a dynamic workload partitioner that assigns tensor operations to a quantum processing unit (QPU) or classical co-processor based on real-time coherence telemetry, a quantum-classical interface bus enabling sub-microsecond handoff, and an on-device reinforcement learning scheduler that optimizes partitioning policy. The invention achieves significant improvements in inference throughput and energy efficiency over classical-only and fixed-partition hybrid approaches, enabling deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

CLAIMS:
Claim 1 [Independent]: A hybrid inference engine comprising: a quantum processing unit (QPU); a classical co-processor; a dynamic workload partitioner configured to assign tensor operations from a neural network inference task to either the QPU or the classical co-processor based on real-time quantum coherence telemetry; and a reinforcement learning scheduler executing on the classical co-processor and configured to update the partitioning policy based on measured latency and energy metrics.

Claim 2 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the dynamic workload partitioner comprises a coherence telemetry module configured to sample QPU T2 relaxation time at intervals not exceeding 100 microseconds.

Claim 3 [Dependent]: The hybrid inference engine of Claim 1, wherein the quantum-classical interface bus supports bidirectional tensor transfer with end-to-end latency not exceeding 800 nanoseconds.

Claim 4 [Independent]: A method of performing neural network inference using a hybrid quantum-classical system, the method comprising: receiving an inference task comprising a plurality of tensor operations; sampling real-time coherence telemetry from a quantum processing unit; assigning each tensor operation to either the quantum processing unit or a classical co-processor using a learned partitioning policy; executing the assigned operations; and updating the partitioning policy using a reinforcement learning reward signal derived from measured inference latency and energy consumption.

PRIOR ART REFERENCES:
Ref. [ID-608302]: Patent. Relevant as prior art teaching static quantum-classical partitioning for matrix multiplication; does not address dynamic reallocation or RL-based scheduling.
Ref. [ID-356290]: Journal Article. Addresses quantum neural networks on NISQ devices; no edge deployment or classical co-processor integration discussed.
Ref. [ID-447248]: Conference Paper. Presents an edge AI accelerator chip with fixed-function classical inference; no quantum co-processing component.

FIGURES:
Fig. 1: System architecture overview showing QPU, classical co-processor, QCI bus, and RL scheduler interconnects.
Fig. 2: Flowchart of the dynamic workload partitioning algorithm.
Fig. 3: Coherence telemetry sampling pipeline and T2 estimation method.
Fig. 4: Comparative throughput benchmark results across three workload types (CNN, Transformer, GNN).
Fig. 5: Energy-per-inference curves comparing the invention against classical-only and fixed-partition baselines.

text:
CLINICAL TRIAL PROTOCOL
Protocol Version 2.1 — Final

Protocol ID: CT-2025-ONCL-99167
Study Title: A Randomized, Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Phase III Trial Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Velaritinib in Patients with Advanced Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)

Sponsor Organization: Meridian BioTherapeutics, Inc.
Principal Investigator: Prof. Sandra K. Hollingsworth, MD, PhD
Affiliation: Mount Calloway Cancer Institute, Seattle, WA

STUDY DESIGN:
This is a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study. Eligible patients will be randomized 2:1 (active:placebo) to receive either Velaritinib 200 mg orally once daily or matched placebo for 24 months or until disease progression, unacceptable toxicity, or withdrawal of consent.

INTERVENTION:
Velaritinib (VLT-200), a novel selective EGFR/MET dual inhibitor, administered as 200 mg oral tablet once daily. Comparator: matching placebo tablet.

TARGET ENROLLMENT: 338 patients across 35 investigational sites in 8 countries.

STUDY DURATION: Approximately 36 months (24-month treatment period + 12-month follow-up).

ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA:
Inclusion:
  • Confirmed diagnosis of advanced non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), Stage IIIB/IV
  • Age 30–80 years
  • ECOG Performance Status 0–2
  • Adequate organ function as per laboratory parameters

Exclusion:
  • Prior treatment with any EGFR or MET inhibitor
  • Active CNS metastases requiring immediate treatment
  • Severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C)

PRIMARY ENDPOINT:
Phase: Efficacy
Metric: Progression-Free Survival (PFS)
Timepoint: 12 months post-randomization

SECONDARY ENDPOINTS include Overall Survival (OS), Objective Response Rate (ORR), Duration of Response (DoR), and Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs).

All procedures are conducted in accordance with ICH E6(R2) Good Clinical Practice guidelines and the Declaration of Helsinki.
